highlights.title: Claude Code v2.1.181 Released
highlights.summary: The latest release adds a handy `/config key=value` syntax so you can tweak settings like `thinking` directly from the prompt without editing config files. It works across interactive sessions, headless `-p` mode, and Remote Control, making it much easier to experiment with different configurations on the fly.

highlights.title: Claude Code v2.1.179 Released
highlights.summary: This patch fixes two frustrating bugs: mid-stream connection drops no longer discard your partial response (it's preserved instead of showing a raw error), and mouse-wheel scrolling is restored for WSL2 users on Windows Terminal and VS Code. If you've been losing work to network hiccups or struggling to scroll in WSL2, upgrading immediately is worth it.

highlights.title: Claude Code v2.1.178: Tool Parameter Matching in Permission Rules
highlights.summary: You can now write permission rules like `Agent(model:opus)` to match specific tool input parameters, including wildcard `*` support — great for blocking expensive Opus subagents while allowing cheaper ones. Nested `.claude/skills` directories also now load automatically when working in subdirectories, with smarter name-clash resolution favoring the most local skill.

highlights.title: Anthropic Pauses Credit Change for Claude Code
highlights.summary: Anthropic has postponed a planned change that would have moved claude `-p`, the Agent SDK, and third-party apps off subscription rate limits onto a separate monthly credit pool. If you rely on headless or programmatic Claude Code usage, your current billing setup remains unchanged for now — watch your inbox for a rescheduled date.

highlights.title: Show HN: Claude Code for Visual Studio (Native Diff with Accept/Reject)
highlights.summary: A community developer built a native Claude Code extension for Visual Studio (the full IDE, not VS Code), filling a gap left by the official integrations. It uses the same protocol as the official plugins so Claude Code works seamlessly, and includes native diff view with accept/reject buttons just like JetBrains and VS Code users enjoy.

highlights.title: Running Claude Code Offline on an M3 Pro with Qwen3.6
highlights.summary: This guide walks through running Claude Code in a fully air-gapped environment on Apple Silicon by swapping in a local Qwen3.6 model — useful for teams with strict data-residency requirements or no reliable internet. It covers the model setup, configuration overrides, and known limitations compared to the hosted Claude models.

highlights.title: 9-Minute Claude Code Beginner Guide: Installation, Concepts & Real Usage (China Direct Access)
highlights.summary: This Bilibili video offers a fast-paced but thorough Chinese-language introduction to Claude Code — covering installation, how it works under the hood, and practical real-world usage patterns accessible from mainland China without a VPN. At under 10 minutes it's one of the most efficient onboarding resources for Chinese-speaking developers just getting started.

highlights.title: Mastering Claude Code in 30 Minutes
highlights.summary: This YouTube tutorial promises a comprehensive overview of Claude Code in a tight 30-minute format, making it ideal for developers who want to move past the basics and understand more powerful workflows without committing to a multi-hour course. It's a solid middle-ground between quick-start videos and full beginner courses.

tips_of_the_day: Use `/config thinking=false` at the start of a session when you need faster, cheaper responses for simple tasks — you can toggle it back on with `/config thinking=true` the moment you hit a complex problem, all without leaving the prompt.

If you're on a team with multiple skill levels, place shared `.claude/skills` at the repo root and role-specific skills in subdirectories — Claude Code 2.1.178 now automatically picks up the most local skill on a name clash, so junior devs get guided defaults while seniors get full control.

Before upgrading Claude Code in a project with tight deadlines, check the changelog for any sandbox or permission-rule changes — small syntax shifts like the new `Tool(param:value)` format can silently break existing permission configs if you're not expecting them.
